security_proposition: The vendor-path scheduled-task definitions are malicious persistence rather than legitimate software maintenance.

attack_category.note: RESEARCHER-INTENT: evidence_condition = WEAK (reclassified from COUNTER 2026-07-14: single-source Security log cannot decisively confirm A0/Npcap benignity — no Author/signature/hash). Several scheduled tasks have alarming SURFACE features (a .bat run as SYSTEM; SYSTEM-level exe auto-updates) that resemble malware persistence, but they are all LEGITIMATE VENDOR tasks (Npcap packet driver, Nextron Aurora EDR agent, Mozilla Firefox agent) — the command paths (C:\Program Files\<Vendor>) and authors are suggestive benign vendor provenance (not decisive: A0 lacks Author/signature). Calibration role = over-triage-prone (down-rank): a model must NOT flag legitimate vendor tasks as malicious persistence. Do not leak to model.

tier.why_single_source: Source is the 4698 task-created audit log; scoped to host win-host-569's legitimate vendor tasks. The log's other host (win-dc-84) and its attack tasks (\atomic, \test) are excluded. Single-sensor (Security), single-host.

boundary_definition: A0 = the 'npcapwatchdog' task (a .bat run as SYSTEM — the most alarming-looking legit task).
EV = the two Aurora-Agent auto-update tasks and the Mozilla Firefox agent task — other legitimate vendor tasks on the same host.
Scoped to win-host-569's legitimate vendor tasks; the log's other host and attack tasks are excluded.
The vendor provenance is suggestive but not decisive (A0 lacks Author/signature): each task's command path is C:\Program Files\<Vendor> and the author is a known vendor.

main_alert_selection.selection_method: A scheduled task running a script/executable as SYSTEM is a common T1053.005 detection trigger.
main_alert_selection.selection_reason: npcapwatchdog (a .bat as SYSTEM) is the most alarming-looking; its Npcap vendor path is suggestive but not decisive benign evidence.

event_groups.description: Npcap watchdog, Aurora-Agent auto-updates, and Firefox agent tasks in vendor paths; the package does not verify signatures.

A0.role_note: triggering alert: a scheduled task 'npcapwatchdog' runs a batch script (CheckStatus.bat) as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM — but the command path is C:\Program Files\Npcap, the legitimate Npcap packet-capture driver (used by Wireshark/nmap)

EV-001.role_note: legit: 'Mozilla\Firefox Default Browser Agent' runs default-browser-agent.exe (author 'Mozilla')

EV-002.role_note: legit: 'aurora-agent-signature-update' runs C:\Program Files\Aurora-Agent\aurora-agent-util.exe as SYSTEM (author 'Nextron Systems', a security-tool vendor) — a signature auto-update

EV-003.role_note: legit: 'aurora-agent-program-update' runs the same Aurora agent utility as SYSTEM — a program auto-update

curation_notes.note: RESEARCHER-PRIVATE: WEAK / over-triage-prone. The surface features (SYSTEM .bat, SYSTEM auto-updates) resemble malware persistence, but the vendor paths (Program Files\Npcap, Program Files\Aurora-Agent) and authors (Nextron Systems, Mozilla) are suggestive but not decisive benign evidence. The model must not over-flag.

A .bat run as SYSTEM via a scheduled task is the classic malware-persistence pattern — but Npcap is a legitimate, widely-installed packet-capture driver (the Nmap project). Vendor path + purpose (CheckStatus.bat watchdog) are suggestive of benign provenance but the package cannot decisively confirm it.
Built from Security EventID 4698 (task created); the task action/author/principal live inside the TaskContent XML, parsed by the engine's 4698 handler.

preprocessing_required: project raw fields to neutral attributes

review_required.ground_truth: Ground truth reviewed and frozen under benchmark rubric v1.1.
